verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: After the exposition of his faith, or rather his lack of knowledge, he passes on to another matter; and tries to make excuses for having turned the books Περὶ ᾽Αρχῶν into Latin.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I will put down his words literally:

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: “I am told that objections have been raised against me because, forsooth, at the request of some of my brethren, I translated certain works of Origen from Greek into Latin.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I suppose that every one sees that it is only through ill-will that this is made a matter of blame.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: For, if there is any offensive statement in the author, why is this to be twisted into a fault of the translator?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I was asked to exhibit in Latin what stands written in the Greek text; and I did nothing more than fit Latin words to Greek ideas.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: If, therefore, there is anything to praise in these ideas, the praise does not belong to me: and similarly as to anything to which blame may attach.”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: “I hear,” he says, “that thence dispute has arisen.”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: How clever this is, to speak of it as a dispute, when it is really an accusation against him.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: “That I have, at the request of my brethren, translated certain things of Origen’s into Latin.”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Yes, but what are these “certain things”?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Have they no name?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Are you silent?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Then the bills of charge brought by the accusers will speak for you.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: “I suppose,” he says, “that every one understands that it is only through envy that these things are made matters of blame.”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Are people envious of your eloquence?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Or have you done what no other man has ever been able to do?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Here am I, who have translated many works of Origen’s; yet, except you, no one shews envy towards me or calumniates me for it.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: “If there is any offensive statement in the author, why is it to be twisted into a fault of the translator?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I was asked to exhibit in Latin what stands written in the Greek text; and I did nothing more than fit Latin words to Greek ideas.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: If, therefore, there is anything to praise in these ideas, the praise does not belong to me, and similarly as to anything to which blame may attach.”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Can you be astonished that men think ill of you when you say of open blasphemies nothing more than, “If there are any offensive statements in the author”?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: What is said in those books is offensive to all men; and you stand alone in your doubt and in your complaint that this is “twisted into a fault of the translator,” when you have praised it in your Preface.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: ‘You were asked to turn it into Latin as it stood in the Greek text.’

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I wish you had done what you pretend you were asked.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You would not then be the object of any ill will.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: If you had kept faith as a translator, it would not have been necessary for me to counteract your false translation by my true one.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You know in your own conscience what you added, what you subtracted, and what you altered on one side or the other at your discretion; and after this you have the audacity to tell us that what is good or evil is not to be attributed to you but to the author.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You shew your sense of the ill will aroused against you by again toning down your words: and as if you were walking with your steps in the air or on the tops of the ears of corn, you say, “Whether there is praise or blame in these opinions.”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You dare not defend him, but you do not choose to condemn him.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Choose which of the two you please; the option is yours; if this which you have translated is good, praise it, if bad, condemn it.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But he makes excuses, and weaves another artifice, He says:

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: “I admit that I put something of my own into the work: as I stated in my Preface, I used my own discretion in cutting out not a few passages; but only those as to which I had come to suspect that the thing had not been so stated by Origen himself, and the statement appeared to me in these cases to have been inserted by others, because in other places I had found the author state the same matter in a catholic sense.”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: What wonderful eloquence!

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Varied, too, with flowers of the Attic style.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: “Moreover also!” and “Things which came to me into suspicion!”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I marvel that he should have dared to send such literary portents to Rome.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: One would think that the man’s tongue was in fetters, and bound with cords that cannot be disentangled, so that it could hardly break forth into human speech.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: However, I will return to the matter in hand.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I wish to know who gave you permission to cut out a number of passages from the work you were translating?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You were asked to turn a Greek book into Latin, not to correct it; to draw out another man’s words, not to write a book of your own.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You confess, by the fact of pruning away so much, that you did not do what you were asked.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: And I wish that what you curtailed had all been the bad parts, and that you had not put in many things of your own which go to support what is bad.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I will take an example, from which men may judge of the rest.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: In the first book of the Περὶ ᾽Αρχῶν where Origen had uttered that impious blasphemy, that the Son does not see the Father, you supply the reasons for this, as if in the name of the writer, and translate the note of Didymus, in which he makes a fruitless effort to defend another man’s error, trying to prove that Origen spoke rightly; but we, poor simple men, like the tame creatures spoken of by Ennius, can understand neither his wisdom nor that of his translator.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Your Preface, which you allege in explanation, in which you flatter and praise me so highly shows you to be guilty of the most serious faults of translation.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You say that you have cut out many things from the Greek, but you say nothing of what you have put in.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Were the parts cut out good or bad?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Bad, I suppose.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Was what you kept good or bad?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Good, I presume; for you could not translate the bad.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Then I suppose you cut off what was bad and left what was good?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But what you have translated can be shewn to be almost wholly bad.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Whatever therefore in your translation I can shew to be bad, must be laid to your account, since you translated it as being good.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: It is a strange thing if you are to act like an unjust censor, who is himself guilty of the crime, and are allowed at your will to expel some from the Senate and keep others in it.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But you say: “It was impossible to change everything.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: I only thought I might cut away what had been added by the heretics.”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Then if you cut away all that you thought had been added by the heretics, all that you left belongs to the work which you were translating.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Answer me then, are these good or bad?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: You could not translate what was bad, since once for all you had cut away what had been added by the heretics, that is, unless you thought it your duty to cut away the bad parts due to the heretics, while translating the errors of Origen himself unaltered into Latin.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Tell me then, why you turned Origen’s heresies into Latin.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: Was it to expose the author of the evil, or to praise him?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: If your object is to expose him, why do you praise him in the Preface?

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: If you praise him you are convicted of being a heretic.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: The only remaining hypothesis is that you published these things as being good.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: But if they are proved to be bad, then author and translator are involved in the same crime, and the Psalmist’s word is fulfilled: “When thou sawest a thief, thou consentedst unto him and hast been partaker with the adulterers.”

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: It is needless to make a plain matter doubtful by arguing about it.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: As to what follows, let him answer whence this suspicion arose in his mind of these additions by heretics.

verses.book: Jerome's Apology for Himself Against the Books of Rufinus.
verses.text: “It was,” he says, “because I found the same things treated by this author in other places in a catholic sense.”
